claims.claim: Lithium-ion batteries (NMC811/SiC, 21700 and 18650 formats) reached venting gas temperatures exceeding 1370°C during thermal runaway, with surface temperatures surpassing 400°C and venting gas velocities exceeding 150 m/s.
claims.quote: Results indicate that the lithium-ion batteries reach venting gas temperatures exceeding 1370 °C, with surface temperatures surpassing 400 °C and venting gas velocities exceeding 150 m s−1.

claims.claim: Sodium-ion batteries (NaMn1/3Fe1/3Ni1/3O2 cathode, 18650 format) exhibited lower temperatures than lithium-ion batteries due to the absence of gas ignition during thermal runaway, with peak venting gas temperatures not exceeding 350°C and all surface temperatures remaining below 400°C.
claims.quote: Conversely, the investigated sodium-ion batteries exhibit lower temperatures due to the absence of gas ignition, precluding the estimation of venting gas velocity... the measured cell surface and venting gas temperatures remained under 400°C and no gas ignition was observed in the tested cells.

claims.claim: Peak mass flow rates during thermal runaway reached 2000 g/s for 18650 LIBs and 1500 g/s for 21700 LIBs, but lasted only several milliseconds, while average mass flow rates ranged between 30 g/s and 300 g/s.
claims.quote: Peak mass flow rates for lithium-ion batteries reach 2000 g s−1, with average rates between 30 g s−1 and 300 g s−1... The peak values of the mass flow rate were even higher - 1500 g s−1 for 21 700 LIBs and 2000 g s−1 for 18 650 LIBs but lasting only several milliseconds.

claims.claim: Total mass loss during thermal runaway ranged from 55% for 18650 sodium-ion batteries to 77% for 18650 lithium-ion batteries, with the lower mass loss in SIBs attributed to lower temperatures, absence of ignition, and lower energy content.
claims.quote: Total mass loss ranges from 55 % in 18650 sodium-ion batteries to 77 % in 18650 lithium-ion batteries... The investigated SIBs have a lower nominal capacity and, consequently, lower stored energy leading to less ejection during TR. No ignition of SIBs was observed in the high-speed videos, which also contributes to the lower mass loss of SIBs.

claims.claim: The TR start temperature of 18650 LIBs ranged between 190°C and 200°C, while the TR start temperature of investigated SIBs was on average 10°C to 15°C higher, indicating greater thermal stability of the investigated SIBs.
claims.quote: The TR start temperature of the 18 650 LIBs ranges between 190°C and 200°C determined based on the cell surface temperature 𝑇surface mid1, with some observed fluctuations between tests... Compared to LIBs, the TR start temperature of investigated SIBs is, on average, 10°C to 15°C higher, and, thus, the TR occurred later despite the same heating rate, indicating that investigated SIBs could be more thermally stable compared to investigated LIBs.

claims.claim: The venting gas velocity of LIBs in both 21700 and 18650 formats exceeded 100 m/s at the beginning of thermal runaway and exhibited exponential decay, falling below 50 m/s after 200 ms and below 25 m/s after 500 ms from the start of the optically visible TR event.
claims.quote: Both LIB formats show similar trends of venting gas velocity over time, exhibiting an exponential decay, as represented in Fig. 4. The high velocities exceeding 100 m s−1 at the beginning of TR can be attributed to the gas ignition. The venting gas velocity decreases after 200 ms, falling below 50 m s−1 in both LIB formats and further drops below the 25 m s−1 value after 500 ms since the beginning of the optically visible TR event.

claims.claim: A lower heating rate combined with a higher SOC leads to greater total mass loss of lithium-ion cells during thermal runaway, due to more homogeneous temperature distribution and simultaneous TR across all cell areas.
claims.quote: Willstrand et al. [6] demonstrated that the lower the heating rate and the higher the SOC level, the greater the total mass loss of the cell. The low heating rate is responsible for a homogeneous temperature distribution within the cell. Consequently, it may lead to simultaneous TR across all cell areas and higher mass loss.

claims.claim: The absence of gas ignition in investigated sodium-ion batteries may be primarily related to the cathode material (NaMn1/3Fe1/3Ni1/3O2), which has better thermal stability than the cathode material of the investigated lithium-ion batteries.
claims.quote: Therefore, the lack of gas ignition in investigated SIBs may be related primarily to the cathode material, which has better thermal stability than the cathode material of LIBs.

claims.claim: The mass flow rate for 18650 SIBs could not be estimated due to the absence of gas ignition and the consequent unavailability of venting gas velocity values.
claims.quote: Notably, the mass flow rate for the 18 650 SIBs could not be estimated due to the absence of ignition and the venting gas velocity values.

claims.claim: The transition time between safety valve opening and thermal runaway event under continuous 128 W heating was on average 128 s for 21700 LIBs, 115 s for 18650 LIBs, and 154 s for 18650 SIBs.
claims.quote: The transition time between the safety valve opening and the TR event with continuous heating at 128 W is, on average, 128 s for 21 700 LIBs, 115 s for 18 650 LIBs and 154 s for 18 650 SIBs.
